commonIssues: Container minimum is set for compact/patio cultivars (e.g. RHS 'Little Black Prince', a 45cm-wide/deep pot, ~40-75L); standard vigorous trailing and semi-erect blackberries are deep-rooted and do far better in-ground or in a 75-114L (20-30 gallon) tub.

Cold hardiness varies sharply by type: trailing cultivars suffer cane and bud damage below about -10C, while erect and semi-erect types are hardier (USDA zones 4-5). The -10C value here is conservative for the less-hardy trailing types.
Trellising is recommended for all types to prevent cane breakage, improve airflow and reduce fruit rot.
Spotted-wing drosophila, Japanese beetles, aphids, spider mites and crown borers are common pests.
Botrytis (grey mould) fruit rot, anthracnose, cane blight, powdery mildew and orange rust are common diseases; full sun and good airflow reduce disease pressure.

source.citation: Clemson Cooperative Extension Home & Garden Information Center — Blackberry: full sun required; apply about 1 inch of water per week if rainfall is insufficient, especially during establishment and fruiting; fertilize in early spring at growth start and again after harvest; optimal soil pH 6.0-6.5; V-trellis recommended; common pests and diseases.
Clemson Cooperative Extension Home & Garden Information Center — Blackberry: fertilize in early spring when growth begins and again after harvest; soil pH 6.0-6.5; V-trellis support recommended.
Clemson Cooperative Extension Home & Garden Information Center — Blackberry: blackberries require full sun for optimal health and production; partial shade leads to more disease and poor yields.

Clemson Cooperative Extension Home & Garden Information Center — Blackberry: V-trellis (8-foot T-posts) recommended to prevent cane breakage and fruit rot; common diseases (anthracnose, cane blight, powdery mildew, botrytis fruit rot, orange rust) and pests (aphids, Japanese beetles, spotted-wing drosophila, spider mites, crown borers).

OSU Extension — A home gardener's guide to growing blackberries: all blackberry types are self-fruitful, so only one cultivar is needed for pollination and fruit; trellising benefits all types; recommended fertilizer such as 10-10-10 in early spring and after harvest.

OSU Extension — Growing blackberries in your home garden / Blackberry cultivars: trailing blackberries are the least cold-hardy, with cane and bud damage when temperatures fall below about 13F (-10.5C); erect and semi-erect types are hardier (USDA zones 4-5).

RHS — How to grow blackberries and hybrid berries: choose a sunny, sheltered site; established plants need little extra water but container-grown plants need regular moisture all season; feed with an organic-based granular general fertiliser in early spring if needed; horizontal support wires ~45cm apart are essential; plant mid-autumn to early spring; harvest mid-summer to early autumn.

RHS — How to grow blackberries and hybrid berries: compact varieties (e.g. 'Little Black Prince') can be grown in a pot at least 45cm wide and deep filled with loam-based compost, reaching ~1m; container plants need regular watering.
Old Farmer's Almanac — Blackberries: Planting, Growing, and Harvesting; full sun, regular watering of about 1 inch per week, trellising recommended for support.

Confidence note — containerProfile: extension/RHS sources give pot guidance only for compact cultivars (RHS: 45cm pot; OSU notes standard blackberries are deep-rooted, ~20-30 gallon / 75-114L tubs). recommendedMinLiters (40L) is a conservative derived minimum for a compact patio cultivar, not a measured value. heatStressC (35C) and feeding intervals (90 days, i.e. early-spring + post-harvest) are derived from qualitative guidance ('fertilize twice a year', 'hot, dry weather') rather than published numbers.
